name: SMART Open-Ended Item Difficulty Prediction Artifact
population_or_context: Open-ended short-answer reading-comprehension and Java coding item datasets with human-scored student responses.
sample_size: Two real-world datasets: 49 short-answer reading-comprehension items and 50 Java coding items, plus thousands of generated simulated responses.
methods: The pipeline learns a simulated student response model from human-scored real responses, aligns generated responses with IRT ability and item difficulty using DPO preference pairs, scores generated responses with an LLM-based scoring model, and fits an IRT model to estimate unseen item difficulty.
education_context: Open-ended educational assessment item development for short-answer reading comprehension and programming.
response_origin: Synthetic open-ended responses generated by an LLM-based simulated student aligned to instructed ability and IRT likelihoods.
rubric_label_space: Open-ended short-answer and coding-response scores used by an LLM-based scoring model and IRT difficulty estimation.
label_source: Human-scored real student responses provide training data, IRT parameters, preference-pair construction, and ground-truth item difficulty estimates.
generation_model: Generative LLM simulator using supervised fine-tuning and DPO; paper reports experiments with small models no larger than 3B parameters.
generation_method: SFT on real student responses followed by DPO preference optimization using response likelihood under a ground-truth IRT model.
prompt_strategy: Prompts include question text, metadata, optional rubric information, and student ability for response simulation and scoring.
evaluation_method: Difficulty-prediction performance against real-response IRT estimates, ability-alignment checks, response feature-distribution comparison, qualitative response analysis, and failure-pattern analysis.
scorer_use: LLM-based scoring model scores generated responses inside an item-difficulty prediction pipeline; the study does not claim operational validation of a final scorer using synthetic responses alone.
real_response_comparator: Human-scored real student responses and IRT-derived item difficulty estimates are the benchmark for generated-response usefulness and response-pattern alignment.
reported_metrics: Table 1 reports SMART difficulty-prediction performance. For Llama-3.2-1B, SMART reaches PCC/SCC/RMSE of 0.6490/0.4861/0.6699 on Smarter Balanced and 0.3082/0.3188/0.6225 on CodeWorkout. For Llama-3.2-3B, SMART reaches 0.6737/0.5661/0.6197 on Smarter Balanced and 0.3929/0.4230/0.5890 on CodeWorkout. Table 2 reports stronger ability alignment for SMART than ZS or SFT baselines.
limitations: The Smarter Balanced dataset is not publicly available.
The number of items in each dataset is small for reliable evaluation.
Generated textual distributions do not always match ground truth and occasionally produce degenerate responses.
Models are no larger than 3B parameters, so results do not settle larger-model comparisons.
